findings.description: hits.pop(0) on a list is O(n) per removal (CPython shifts all elements); the per-client bucket should be a collections.deque using popleft() for O(1) amortized sliding-window pruning.

Backing store is list (defaultdict(list)); the entire sliding-window pattern here (append-tail / pop-head) is the canonical use case for deque, and switching it eliminates the quadratic pop(0) cost on the hot check() path.

current_usage() does an O(n) scan and reads expired timestamps without ever pruning them, so buckets touched only by current_usage()/check() rejections never shrink and grow unbounded until a sweep().

self._hits[key] on a defaultdict(list) inserts a fresh empty bucket on every read (lines 39/54/57), so merely querying a novel client key permanently grows the dict between sweeps — the read path allocates, not just the write path.
sweep() is O(N*limit) and only deletes buckets that are entirely empty, so a bucket with one live hit plus many un-pruned dead timestamps is scanned every sweep yet never reclaimed or compacted.

Off-by-one: 'len(hits) <= self.limit' admits and appends at the boundary, letting the bucket reach limit+1 and allowing one request over the intended cap (deferred to critic-logic; flagged here because the bucket size drives pop(0) cost).
